skills.skill: 1.1 Introducing Statistics: What Can We Learn from Data?
skills.prompt: This topic introduces statistical thinking for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data. Key concepts: variation is natural and expected in data; statistical methods help distinguish real patterns from random variation; data is collected to answer questions about the world; individuals are the objects described by data, and variables are characteristics measured on individuals. Types of studies: observational studies (observe without intervention) and experiments (impose treatments). Statistics provides tools for making informed decisions under uncertainty. AP exam note: students should understand that statistics is fundamentally about managing variation and making evidence-based conclusions. The investigative process (question, data, analysis, conclusion) structures statistical work. Vocabulary: statistics, data, variation, individual, variable, observational study, experiment, inference.

This topic covers types of variables for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-1.B, VAR-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Variables are classified as categorical (assign labels/categories, like color or gender) or quantitative (take numerical values that can be ordered and measured, like height or income). Quantitative variables are further classified as discrete (countable values, like number of siblings) or continuous (any value in an interval, like weight). Key concepts: the type of variable determines the appropriate graphs, summaries, and analyses; categorical data uses counts and proportions; quantitative data uses means, medians, and standard deviations. Students should classify variables correctly from contextual descriptions. AP exam note: correctly identifying variable type is the first step in any statistical analysis. Using the wrong methods for the variable type leads to meaningless results. Vocabulary: categorical variable, quantitative variable, discrete, continuous, classify, numerical, proportion, count.

skills.skill: 1.3 Representing a Categorical Variable with Tables
skills.prompt: This topic covers frequency and relative frequency tables for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). A frequency table shows counts of individuals in each category; a relative frequency table shows proportions (frequency/total). Key concepts: relative frequencies allow comparison between groups of different sizes; marginal frequencies are row or column totals in two-way tables; the sum of all relative frequencies equals 1. Students should construct one-way frequency tables and compute relative frequencies (proportions and percentages). Tables organize categorical data for analysis and are the basis for bar charts and other displays. AP exam note: students should be able to read, construct, and interpret frequency tables, using both counts and proportions as appropriate. Vocabulary: frequency table, relative frequency, proportion, count, category, marginal frequency, total.

skills.skill: 1.4 Representing a Categorical Variable with Graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers graphical displays of categorical data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.C, UNC-1.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Bar charts display frequencies or relative frequencies with bars for each category (bars should not touch, as categories are discrete). Pie charts show each category as a proportion of the whole. Key concepts: bar charts are more flexible and easier to read than pie charts; bars can be ordered by frequency (Pareto chart) for clarity; each bar's height represents frequency or relative frequency; compare distributions using side-by-side or segmented bar charts. Students should choose appropriate graphs and describe what they reveal about the distribution. AP exam note: students should construct and interpret bar charts and describe distributions of categorical variables in context. Vocabulary: bar chart, pie chart, frequency, relative frequency, distribution, Pareto chart, segmented bar chart.

skills.skill: 1.5 Representing a Quantitative Variable with Graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers graphical displays of quantitative data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.E, UNC-1.F, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Common displays: dotplots (each value as a dot), histograms (bars showing frequency for intervals), and stemplots (stem-and-leaf displays). Key concepts: histograms group data into bins (intervals) of equal width; the y-axis shows frequency or relative frequency; bin width affects the appearance (too wide hides detail, too narrow creates noise); stemplots preserve actual data values while showing shape. Students should construct and interpret these displays. The choice of display depends on the data size and purpose. AP exam note: students should describe the distribution in terms of shape, center, spread, and unusual features (SOCS). Context is required. Vocabulary: dotplot, histogram, stemplot, bin width, frequency, shape, distribution, quantitative.

skills.skill: 1.6 Describing the Distribution of a Quantitative Variable
skills.prompt: This topic covers describing distributions of quantitative data for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.G, UNC-1.H, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students describe distributions using SOCS: Shape (symmetric, skewed left/right, unimodal/bimodal/uniform), Outliers (values far from the rest), Center (mean, median), and Spread (range, IQR, standard deviation). Key concepts: skewed right means tail extends to the right (mean > median); skewed left means tail extends left (mean < median); symmetric means approximately equal on both sides (mean ~ median); clusters and gaps are notable features. All descriptions must be in context (referring to the actual variable and units). AP exam note: a complete description addresses all four SOCS components in context. Omitting context or any SOCS component costs points on free-response questions. Vocabulary: shape, center, spread, outlier, skewed, symmetric, unimodal, bimodal, SOCS, context.

skills.skill: 1.7 Summary Statistics for a Quantitative Variable
skills.prompt: This topic covers summary statistics for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.I, UNC-1.J, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). Measures of center: mean (x-bar = sum/n, sensitive to outliers) and median (middle value, resistant to outliers). Measures of spread: range (max-min), interquartile range (IQR = Q3-Q1, resistant), standard deviation (s, measures average distance from mean, sensitive to outliers), variance (s^2). Key concepts: the mean and standard deviation are paired (both affected by outliers); the median and IQR are paired (both resistant); choosing resistant measures (median, IQR) is appropriate for skewed data or data with outliers; the standard deviation is zero only when all values are the same. Students should compute and interpret all measures. AP exam note: interpretations must be in context. 'The standard deviation of 3.2 inches means the heights typically vary about 3.2 inches from the mean.' Vocabulary: mean, median, range, IQR, standard deviation, variance, resistant, sensitive, outlier, center, spread.

This topic covers boxplots and other graphical summaries for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.K, UNC-1.L, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). A boxplot displays the five-number summary: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum. Modified boxplots show outliers as individual points beyond the fences (Q1-1.5IQR and Q3+1.5IQR). Key concepts: the box spans the IQR (middle 50%); the line in the box is the median; whiskers extend to the smallest/largest non-outlier values; boxplots are useful for comparing distributions side by side; they show center, spread, and skewness but not shape details (modes, gaps). Students should construct and interpret boxplots and use the 1.5*IQR rule to identify outliers. AP exam note: side-by-side boxplots require comparative language (group A has a higher median than group B). Vocabulary: boxplot, five-number summary, quartile, IQR, whisker, outlier, fence, 1.5IQR rule, modified boxplot.

skills.skill: 1.9 Comparing Distributions of a Quantitative Variable
skills.prompt: This topic covers comparing distributions for AP Statistics (CED: UNC-1.M, UNC-1.N, UNC-1.O, Unit 1, Topic 1.9). When comparing two or more distributions, students should address shape, center, spread, and outliers for each, using comparative language. Key concepts: use side-by-side boxplots, back-to-back stemplots, or parallel dotplots for visual comparison; compare centers (which group has higher typical values), spreads (which is more variable), shapes (both symmetric? one skewed?), and note unusual features. All comparisons must use context and comparative words (greater than, less than, similar to). Students should avoid listing features separately without comparing. AP exam note: comparison questions require explicit comparative language. Saying 'Group A has mean 5, Group B has mean 3' without stating 'Group A has a higher mean' is incomplete. Vocabulary: compare, distribution, center, spread, shape, comparative language, side-by-side, variation, context.

This topic covers the normal distribution for AP Statistics (CED: VAR-2.A, VAR-2.B, VAR-2.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.10). The normal distribution is bell-shaped, symmetric, and characterized by mean (mu) and standard deviation (sigma). Key concepts: the empirical rule (68-95-99.7): approximately 68% of data within 1 sigma, 95% within 2 sigma, 99.7% within 3 sigma; z-scores standardize: z = (x - mu)/sigma, giving the number of standard deviations from the mean; the standard normal N(0,1) is the reference distribution; use z-tables or technology to find proportions/percentiles. Students should calculate z-scores, find proportions above/below/between values, and find values given proportions (inverse normal). Assessing normality: use normal probability plots (straight line suggests normality). AP exam note: normal distribution calculations are foundational for inference. Students must show the z-score computation and reference the normal distribution. Vocabulary: normal distribution, bell curve, mean, standard deviation, z-score, empirical rule, percentile, standard normal.
